file_name: Bian et al. - 2022 - Skin lesion image classification method based on extension theory and deep learning.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning, specifically a model named YoDyCK (YOLOv3 optimized by Dynamic Convolution Kernel), to classify skin lesion images into three classes: pigmented nevi, nail matrix nevi, and malignant melanoma. It also employs extension theory to detect the Blue White Veil (BWV) feature, which is integrated with the deep learning features for the classification task.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel two-phase classification method for skin lesion images integrating extension theory and deep learning (YoDyCK). It details the methodology, presents experimental results comparing the proposed method against state-of-the-art techniques, and reports specific performance metrics (e.g., accuracy of 0.92 for the first phase and 0.93 for the second phase), indicating original experimental research rather than a literature review.

title: Skin lesion image classification method based on extension theory and deep learning
brief_summary: This study proposes a two-phase classification method for skin lesion images targeting pigmented nevi, nail matrix nevi, and malignant melanoma, specifically for Asian skin types. The approach integrates medical domain knowledge with deep learning by using extension theory to detect the Blue White Veil (BWV) feature and a novel YoDyCK model (YOLOv3 optimized by Dynamic Convolution Kernel) for feature extraction. The method was evaluated on the ISBI 2016 dataset and a custom dataset of 300 dermoscopic images collected from a local hospital. Performance was assessed using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics, comparing the proposed method against state-of-the-art techniques like Faster-RCNN and various ensemble methods. The results demonstrated that the proposed two-phase method achieved superior classification accuracy of 0.93 on the custom dataset and 0.962 on the ISBI 2016 dataset, outperforming existing methods while reducing training time.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three final classification classes in the second phase. Included in the custom dataset (150 images) and ISBI 2016 dataset. Classified as benign in the first phase.
One of the three final classification classes in the second phase. Included in the custom dataset (100 images). Classified as benign in the first phase.
One of the three final classification classes in the second phase. Included in the custom dataset (50 images) and ISBI 2016 dataset. Classified as suspected malignant in the first phase.
Intermediate category in the first phase of the two-phase classification method, grouping pigmented nevi and nail matrix nevi before final classification.
Intermediate category in the first phase of the two-phase classification method, primarily containing malignant melanoma cases, processed by the YoDyCK model in the second phase.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion diagnostic categories (benign vs. suspected malignant in phase 1; pigmented nevi, nail matrix nevi, and malignant melanoma in phase 2)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a two-phase classification method. Phase 1 classifies images into 'benign skin lesion images' and 'suspected malignant skin lesion images' using extension theory. Phase 2 classifies benign images into 'pigmented nevi' and 'nail matrix nevi' using YOLOv3, and suspected malignant images into 'pigmented nevi', 'nail matrix nevi', and 'malignant melanoma' using the YoDyCK model. The task is explicitly defined as image classification with discrete diagnostic labels.

datasets_used.name: ISBI 2016
datasets_used.description: A public dataset created for the Skin Lesion Analysis Toward Melanoma Detection challenge. Used for external evaluation and comparison against state-of-the-art methods.

use.testing: Used for testing and performance comparison of the proposed two-phase classification method.

datasets_used.name: Custom Hospital Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset collected from a well-known local hospital, containing dermoscopic images with pixel resolution 768 × 576. Images were labeled by two experienced dermatologists. Images with lesions blocked by hair/oil or located on palms/soles/lips/private areas were excluded. Noise such as hair and marks was removed using an inpainting method.

use.training: Used for training the YoDyCK and YOLOv3 models (specific split not reported, but used for model development).
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed method, achieving an accuracy of 0.93.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reports accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics and states that the proposed method 'significantly improves' classification accuracy compared to state-of-the-art methods, but does not provide statistical significance testing results.

data_augmentation_comparison.notes: Table 2 details per-class accuracy scores and confidence intervals for benign vs malignant classification under both augmentation methods.

first_phase_classification.notes: Classified 300 images into benign (176) and suspected malignant (124). TP=100, FP=24, FN=0, TN=176.

first_phase_comparison.notes: Comparison of methods based on different features for malignant melanoma classification.

second_phase_classification.notes: Table 5 shows detailed counts: Pigmented nevi (90 correct, 1 unable), Nail matrix nevi (76 correct, 4 misclassified, 5 unable), Malignant melanoma (92 correct, 8 unable).

comparison_with_baselines.notes: Training time of 'Our method' was 56.4 min faster than 'ours-Yodyck-only'.
Results from Table 6 comparing proposed method against state-of-the-art methods on the ISBI 2016 dataset.

architecture_used.main_model: YoDyCK (YOLOv3 optimized by Dynamic Convolution Kernel) and YOLOv3
architecture_used.pretraining: Pretraining weights given in YOLOv3 were used to accelerate training speed.

architecture_details.optimization: Dynamic Convolution Kernel with gradually decreasing size (starting at 11x11, reducing to 3x3)
architecture_details.components: DBL: Darknetconv2d_BN_Leaky is the basic component

classification_or_output_head.type: Bounding box prediction and class conditional probabilities

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: YoDyCK (YOLOv3 optimized by Dynamic Convolution Kernel)
preprocessing_techniques: Exclusion of images with lesions blocked by hair/oil or located on palms/soles/lips/private areas
Noise removal (hair and marks) using an inpainting method
RGB to B-G color channel transformation to enhance Blue White Veil (BWV) features
Average pooling (3x3 kernel, step size 1) to reduce noise, dimensionality, and summarize features

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to original images including image scaling (up and down), rotation, and flipping. Used to increase the number of images in the dataset.

Used to generate fake images to balance the dataset and increase diversity. Training iterations were set to 3000. Resulted in 300 images per class (pigmented nevus, nail matrix nevus, melanoma) after augmentation.

conclusions: The proposed two-phase classification method, which integrates medical domain knowledge (extension theory) with deep learning (YoDyCK), significantly improves classification accuracy for skin lesions compared to state-of-the-art methods.
The method achieves an accuracy of 0.93 on a custom dataset of 300 dermoscopic images and 0.962 on the ISBI 2016 dataset.
Integrating the Blue White Veil (BWV) feature detection via extension theory allows for effective preliminary classification of skin lesions into benign and suspected malignant categories.

The YoDyCK model (YOLOv3 optimized by Dynamic Convolution Kernel) effectively extracts deep learning features from suspected malignant skin lesion images, reducing training time and improving accuracy.
Data augmentation using WGAN (Wasserstein GAN) is more effective than geometric methods for improving classification accuracy, particularly for malignant melanoma.
The performance of the YOLOv3 model improves as the training set size increases, but the influence becomes insignificant when the number of images exceeds 900.
A step-based decay learning rate with a factor of approximately 0.33 (1/3) was found to be optimal for training the model weights.

The proposed method is specifically designed for and effective in classifying skin diseases in Asians, addressing the limitation that most existing methods do not well integrate medical domain features with deep learning.
important_remarks: The custom dataset is extremely small (300 images total: 150 pigmented nevi, 100 nail matrix nevi, 50 malignant melanoma), raising concerns about statistical power and generalizability.
The study focuses specifically on Asian skin types, limiting the external validity of the results to other ethnicities.
The custom dataset was collected from a single local hospital, introducing potential selection bias and lack of diversity in imaging equipment or patient demographics.
No formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals for the final comparison) was reported; claims of 'significant improvement' are based solely on point estimates.

The paper does not report the specific train/validation/test split for the custom dataset, only that 300 images were used for testing, which may imply data leakage or lack of a held-out validation set for hyperparameter tuning.

The ISBI 2016 dataset was used for external evaluation, but the paper does not specify if the model was trained on any part of ISBI 2016 or if it was purely zero-shot/fine-tuned, making the comparison context ambiguous.

The method relies on the detection of the Blue White Veil (BWV) feature via extension theory; if BWV is absent or poorly defined in a lesion, the preliminary classification may fail, potentially affecting the second phase's input.

The custom dataset excluded images with lesions on palms, soles, lips, or private areas, and those blocked by hair/oil, which limits the clinical applicability to common dermoscopic scenarios but excludes other important diagnostic contexts.
The 'unable to classify' cases in the second phase (e.g., 9 malignant melanomas unable to classify) indicate a failure mode of the YoDyCK model that is not fully addressed in the accuracy metric.
Data augmentation relied heavily on WGAN (3000 iterations) to balance classes, which may introduce synthetic artifacts or overfitting to generated features rather than real biological variance.
